```{r}
tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/Claude/analysis_non_post_secondary_grads_ct/High school accomplishments/ACTCompositeScore-5.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)

```

#### grad.year.10

```{r}
summary <- aov(ACTCompositeScore ~ grad.year.10, data = data.10)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/Claude/analysis_non_post_secondary_grads_ct/High school accomplishments/ACTCompositeScore-10.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)

```
:::

<br>

### `ap.exam` 

AP exam participation shows a statistically significant association with workforce participation outcomes at all three time points, with Cramer's V declining substantially over time (V=0.149 at year 1, V=0.101 at year 5, V=0.065 at year 10). It is worth noting that AP exam takers represent a small minority of this non-college completer population — 1,399 of 20,795 at year 1 — which reflects the inherent tension in this variable: AP participation is strongly associated with academic orientation and postsecondary pursuit, making it somewhat unusual to observe in a non-completer population.

At year 1, the dominant finding is that AP exam takers are far more likely to still be attending postsecondary (44.4% vs. 20.2%), a gap of 24 percentage points — the largest postsecondary attendance gap observed for any variable in this analysis. This reflects the strong selection of AP participants toward postsecondary continuation, even among those who ultimately do not complete a credential. AP participants show substantially lower sustained regional employment (10.8% vs. 19.9%), lower no MN employment record rates (13.4% vs. 21.9%), and lower non-sustained workforce participation (25.9% vs. 31.6%), all consistent with their concentration in postsecondary attendance at year 1.

By year 5, postsecondary attendance remains notably elevated among AP participants (10.1% vs. 4.1%), again the largest such gap at year 5 of any variable examined. The most distinctive labor market difference is in sustained statewide employment, where AP participants show a rate of 21.5% compared to 14.6% among non-participants — a gap of nearly seven percentage points consistent with the academic pathway pattern. Sustained regional employment is substantially lower among AP participants (16.3% vs. 26.4%), while no MN employment record rates are nearly identical across groups (30.3% vs. 30.8%).

At year 10, the association has weakened considerably (V=0.065), with the most notable remaining difference being in sustained statewide employment — 24.9% for AP participants versus 17.4% for non-participants — and sustained regional employment, which remains lower for AP participants (18.1% vs. 25.3%). No MN employment record rates are similar and slightly higher for AP participants (41.0% vs. 38.4%), consistent with a pattern of greater geographic dispersion or out-of-state employment among this academically oriented subgroup.

Overall, AP exam participation shows a pattern nearly identical in character to ACT participation and PSEO participation: higher postsecondary attendance early, substantially lower regional employment across all time points, and persistently higher statewide employment among those who do enter the labor market. The year 10 attenuation of the overall effect size reflects convergence in no MN employment record rates and non-sustained workforce participation, while the regional versus statewide employment divide remains the most durable signature of AP participation among non-college completers.

::: panel-tabset
#### grad.year.1

```{r}
tab_xtab(var.row = data.1$grad.year.1,
         var.col = data.1$ap.exam,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/Claude/analysis_non_post_secondary_grads_ct/High school accomplishments/ap-exam-1.html")


```

#### grad.year.5

```{r}
tab_xtab(var.row = data.5$grad.year.5,
         var.col = data.5$ap.exam,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/Claude/analysis_non_post_secondary_grads_ct/High school accomplishments/ap-exam-5.html")


```

#### grad.year.10

```{r}
tab_xtab(var.row = data.10$grad.year.10,
         var.col = data.10$ap.exam,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/Claude/analysis_non_post_secondary_grads_ct/High school accomplishments/ap-exam-10.html")


```
:::

<br>

### `MCA.M`

MCA Mathematics scores show a statistically significant association with workforce participation outcomes at all three time points, with F-statistics declining over time (F=89.4 at year 1, F=36.0 at year 5, F=34.6 at year 10). Score differences across outcome categories are modest in magnitude — generally in the range of 0.1 to 0.4 scaled score units — but consistent in direction and statistically robust.

At year 1, postsecondary attendees show the highest mean MCA Math scores, approximately 0.26 points above sustained regional employment and 0.34 points above non-sustained workforce participation. Among those already in the labor market, sustained statewide employment shows scores 0.13 points above sustained regional employment, while non-sustained workers and those with no MN employment record show scores roughly 0.08 to 0.11 points below sustained regional employment — though these two lower categories do not differ significantly from each other. The overall pattern at year 1 positions sustained regional employment as a middle category, with higher scores associated with postsecondary attendance and statewide employment, and lower scores associated with non-sustained work and labor market absence.

By year 5, the gradient sharpens. Sustained statewide employment now shows scores 0.25 points above sustained regional employment, and postsecondary attendees remain the highest-scoring group (0.28 points above regional). A notable change from year 1 is that the no MN employment record group has shifted upward relative to sustained regional employment — now scoring 0.07 points above regional employment and significantly so — while non-sustained workers remain below regional (−0.04 points, not significant). Sustained statewide employment and postsecondary attendees are statistically indistinguishable from each other at year 5, both sitting well above all other categories.

At year 10, the pattern consolidates further. Sustained statewide employment shows the highest mean scores, 0.29 points above sustained regional employment — the largest regional-to-statewide gap across all three time points. The no MN employment record group continues its upward trajectory relative to regional employment (0.18 points above, significant), now clearly distinguishable from both sustained regional and non-sustained workers. Sustained regional employment and non-sustained workforce participation remain statistically indistinguishable from each other at year 10, together forming the lowest-scoring labor market group. Sustained statewide employment and no MN employment record are also statistically distinguishable (−0.12 points), with statewide employment scoring higher.

Overall, MCA Math scores produce a pattern consistent with the other academic achievement variables: higher scores are associated with out-of-region statewide employment and — increasingly over time — with absence from Minnesota employment records, while sustained regional employment and non-sustained workforce participation are associated with the lowest scores. The growing separation of the no MN employment record group from regional and non-sustained workers over time mirrors the ACT Composite Score findings and reinforces the interpretation that higher-achieving non-college completers are increasingly likely to be employed outside Minnesota by year 10.

::: panel-tabset
#### grad.year.1

```{r}
summary <- aov(MCA.M ~ grad.year.1, data = data.1)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/Claude/analysis_non_post_secondary_grads_ct/High school accomplishments/MCA-M-1.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)

```

#### grad.year.5

```{r}
summary <- aov(MCA.M ~ grad.year.5, data = data.5)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/Claude/analysis_non_post_secondary_grads_ct/High school accomplishments/MCA-M-5.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)

```

#### grad.year.10

```{r}
summary <- aov(MCA.M ~ grad.year.10, data = data.10)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/Claude/analysis_non_post_secondary_grads_ct/High school accomplishments/MCA-M-10.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)

```
:::

<br>

### `MCA.R`

MCA Reading scores show a statistically significant association with workforce participation outcomes at all three time points, with the strongest F-statistic of any continuous variable examined thus far at year 1 (F=144.3), declining over time (F=42.0 at year 5, F=28.0 at year 10). Score differences across outcome categories are modest in absolute terms — generally 0.1 to 0.5 scaled score units — but the gradient is clear, consistent, and statistically robust throughout.

At year 1, postsecondary attendees show the highest mean MCA Reading scores by a wide margin — 0.40 points above sustained regional employment, 0.25 points above sustained statewide employment, and 0.37 to 0.48 points above non-sustained workers and those with no MN employment record. Among those already in the labor market, sustained statewide employment scores 0.15 points above sustained regional employment, while non-sustained workers are statistically indistinguishable from sustained regional workers. The no MN employment record group shows the lowest mean scores of any category, falling 0.08 points below sustained regional employment and 0.11 points below non-sustained workers — a notable contrast with MCA Math, where the no MN record group and non-sustained workers were similarly positioned at year 1.

By year 5, the ordering of categories has shifted in a pattern now familiar from ACT and MCA Math. Sustained statewide employment scores 0.25 points above sustained regional employment, and postsecondary attendees remain the highest-scoring group (0.38 points above regional). The no MN employment record group has moved upward relative to sustained regional employment — now scoring 0.10 points above regional and statistically distinguishable — while non-sustained workers remain essentially identical to sustained regional employment (0.01 points, not significant). Sustained statewide employment and postsecondary attendees both sit well above non-sustained workers and regional employment, consistent with the academic pathway pattern.

At year 10, the structure simplifies into a clear three-tier pattern. Sustained statewide employment shows the highest mean scores (0.25 points above regional), the no MN employment record group is the next highest (0.21 points above regional), and sustained regional and non-sustained workforce participation are statistically indistinguishable from each other — forming the lowest-scoring tier. The no MN employment record and sustained statewide groups are also statistically indistinguishable from each other at year 10 (−0.04 points), together constituting a distinct upper tier of MCA Reading performance among non-college completers.

The MCA Reading pattern closely mirrors MCA Math across all three time points, with the shared signature that regional employment and non-sustained workforce participation occupy the lowest achievement tier, while statewide employment and no MN employment record increasingly converge into a higher- scoring group over time. The F-statistics for MCA Reading are uniformly higher than for MCA Math at every time point, suggesting that reading achievement carries somewhat stronger differentiation across workforce participation categories in this population — consistent with its role as the primary academic achievement indicator retained in the full population multivariate models.

::: panel-tabset
#### grad.year.1

```{r}
summary <- aov(MCA.R ~ grad.year.1, data = data.1)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/Claude/analysis_non_post_secondary_grads_ct/High school accomplishments/MCA-R-1.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)

```

#### grad.year.5

```{r}
summary <- aov(MCA.R ~ grad.year.5, data = data.5)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/Claude/analysis_non_post_secondary_grads_ct/High school accomplishments/MCA-R-5.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)

```

#### grad.year.10

```{r}
summary <- aov(MCA.R ~ grad.year.10, data = data.10)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/Claude/analysis_non_post_secondary_grads_ct/High school accomplishments/MCA-R-10.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)

```
:::

<br>

### `MCA.S`

MCA Science scores show a statistically significant association with workforce participation outcomes at all three time points, with F-statistics declining over time (F=84.4 at year 1, F=38.1 at year 5, F=25.1 at year 10). The pattern of differences across outcome categories is strikingly consistent with MCA Reading and MCA Math, reinforcing the broader finding that academic achievement across subjects produces a common gradient in workforce participation outcomes among non-college completers.

At year 1, postsecondary attendees show the highest mean science scores, 0.30 points above sustained regional employment and 0.13 points above sustained statewide employment. Among those in the labor market, sustained statewide employment scores 0.17 points above sustained regional employment. Non-sustained workers and those with no MN employment record are statistically indistinguishable from sustained regional employment at year 1 — a pattern similar to MCA Math and consistent with the early-stage sorting that has not yet fully differentiated these groups by academic achievement.

By year 5, the structure sharpens in a now-familiar way

::: panel-tabset
#### grad.year.1

```{r}
summary <- aov(MCA.S ~ grad.year.1, data = data.1)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/Claude/analysis_non_post_secondary_grads_ct/High school accomplishments/MCA-S-1.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)
```

#### grad.year.5

```{r}
summary <- aov(MCA.S ~ grad.year.5, data = data.5)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/Claude/analysis_non_post_secondary_grads_ct/High school accomplishments/MCA-S-5.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)
```

#### grad.year.10

```{r}
summary <- aov(MCA.S ~ grad.year.10, data = data.10)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/Claude/analysis_non_post_secondary_grads_ct/High school accomplishments/MCA-S-10.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)
```
:::

<br>

### `cte.achievement`

CTE achievement shows a statistically significant association with workforce participation outcomes at all three time points, with Cramer's V relatively stable and modest across the observation windows (V=0.094 at year 1, V=0.106 at year 5, V=0.115 at year 10). Unlike the academic achievement variables examined previously, CTE achievement produces a pattern oriented toward regional rather than statewide employment, consistent with the CTE pathway identified in the full population analysis.

At year 1, the three CTE categories show a clear gradient in sustained regional employment: CTE concentrators or completers show the highest rate (22.0%), followed by CTE participants (18.3%), and those with no CTE engagement show the lowest rate (13.5%). This monotonic relationship — stronger CTE engagement associated with higher regional employment — is the defining feature of this variable and stands in direct contrast to the pattern observed for academic achievement variables. The no CTE group shows the highest no MN employment record rate (30.5%), more than 11 percentage points above CTE concentrators (18.9%) and participants (19.1%). Postsecondary attendance rates are similar across all three CTE categories at year 1 (21.5%, 23.1%, 21.0%), indicating that CTE engagement does not meaningfully differentiate early postsecondary continuation in this non-completer population.

By year 5, the regional employment gradient has sharpened considerably. CTE concentrators show a sustained regional employment rate of 30.0% — nearly 12.5 percentage points above the no CTE group (17.5%) and 5 points above CTE participants (25.0%). The no CTE group continues to show by far the highest no MN employment record rate (41.9%), roughly 15 points above CTE concentrators (26.6%) and 13 points above CTE participants (29.1%). Sustained statewide employment rates are similar across CTE categories (15.2%, 15.8%, 13.9%), indicating that CTE engagement does not meaningfully differentiate out-of-region labor market mobility — its primary signature is regional attachment, not broader geographic reach.

At year 10, the pattern is most pronounced. CTE concentrators show a sustained regional employment rate of 29.4%, compared to 23.8% for participants and 14.4% for the no CTE group — a gap of 15 percentage points between the highest and lowest CTE categories that represents the largest category-level difference for any variable examined in this group. The no CTE group shows an extraordinarily high no MN employment record rate of 51.5%, compared to 38.1% for CTE participants and 33.6% for CTE concentrators. Statewide employment rates remain similar across categories (18.2%, 18.6%, 16.5%), again confirming that CTE achievement is specifically associated with regional rather than statewide employment outcomes.

Overall, CTE achievement is among the stronger and most interpretable predictors of workforce participation in this population. Its signature is the mirror image of the academic achievement variables: stronger CTE engagement is associated with higher sustained regional employment and lower rates of MN employment record absence, while the no CTE group shows the most adverse profile on both dimensions across all three time points. The effect size grows modestly but consistently over time, suggesting that the regional labor market advantage associated with CTE engagement becomes more entrenched as graduates settle into stable employment patterns.

::: panel-tabset
#### grad.year.1

```{r}
tab_xtab(var.row = data.1$grad.year.1,
         var.col = data.1$cte.achievement,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/Claude/analysis_non_post_secondary_grads_ct/High school accomplishments/cte-achievement-1.html")
```

#### grad.year.5

```{r}
tab_xtab(var.row = data.5$grad.year.5,
         var.col = data.5$cte.achievement,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/Claude/analysis_non_post_secondary_grads_ct/High school accomplishments/cte-achievement-5.html")

```

#### grad.year.10

```{r}
tab_xtab(var.row = data.10$grad.year.10,
         var.col = data.10$cte.achievement,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/Claude/analysis_non_post_secondary_grads_ct/High school accomplishments/cte-achievement-10.html")

```
:::

<br>

### `total.cte.courses.taken`

Total CTE courses taken shows a statistically significant association with workforce participation outcomes at all three time points, with among the highest F-statistics of any variable in the high school accomplishments group (F=146.8 at year 1, F=101.3 at year 5, F=62.1 at year 10). Unlike Cramer's V, F-statistics are not bounded and reflect both the magnitude of mean differences and sample size, so these values indicate a strong and consistent signal. The direction of the effect is the opposite of the academic achievement variables: higher CTE course-taking is consistently associated with sustained regional employment, while lower CTE course-taking is associated with absence from Minnesota employment records.

At year 1, sustained regional employment is associated with the highest mean number of CTE courses, with all other categories showing meaningfully fewer courses. Non-sustained workers show 1.03 fewer courses than sustained regional workers, postsecondary attendees show 1.22 fewer, and the no MN employment record group shows the fewest — 1.92 fewer courses than sustained regional workers, by far the largest gap at year 1. Sustained statewide employment is statistically indistinguishable from sustained regional employment (−0.13 courses, not significant), the only category that matches regional workers in CTE course-taking. This early pattern establishes CTE course volume as a strong differentiator between regional labor market engagement and non-engagement, with the no MN employment record group showing particularly low CTE course-taking.

By year 5, the structure is largely preserved but with some shifts. Sustained regional employment continues to show the highest mean CTE courses, with all other categories significantly below. Sustained statewide employment has now separated from sustained regional workers (−0.69 courses, significant), confirming that out-of-region employment is associated with fewer CTE courses even relative to year 1. Non-sustained workers show 0.82 fewer courses than regional workers, and the no MN employment record group remains the furthest below (−1.60 courses). Postsecondary attendees and the no MN record group are statistically indistinguishable from each other at year 5, both showing very low CTE course volumes.

At year 10, the pattern consolidates into a clear ordering. Sustained regional employment maintains the highest mean CTE courses, with sustained statewide employment (−0.90 courses) and non-sustained workforce participation (−0.86 courses) both significantly below and statistically indistinguishable from each other — forming a middle tier. The no MN employment record group shows the lowest CTE course-taking at year 10 (−1.40 courses below regional), significantly below all other categories including non-sustained workers (−0.55 courses below non-sustained). This creates a clear three- tier structure at year 10 that mirrors the CTE achievement categorical findings: regional employment at the top, statewide employment and non-sustained workers in the middle, and the no MN employment record group at the bottom.

Overall, total CTE courses taken produces the strongest F-statistics of any variable in the high school accomplishments group and shows the most consistent and durable advantage for sustained regional employment across all three time points. Its pattern directly complements CTE achievement: both variables point to CTE engagement as a robust and growing predictor of regional labor market attachment among non-college completers, with the no MN employment record group consistently showing the lowest CTE engagement at every observation window.

::: panel-tabset
#### grad.year.1

```{r}
summary <- aov(total.cte.courses.taken ~ grad.year.1, data = data.1)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/Claude/analysis_non_post_secondary_grads_ct/High school accomplishments/total-cte-courses-taken-1.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)

```

#### grad.year.5

```{r}
summary <- aov(total.cte.courses.taken ~ grad.year.5, data = data.5)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/Claude/analysis_non_post_secondary_grads_ct/High school accomplishments/total-cte-courses-taken-5.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)

```

#### grad.year.10

```{r}
summary <- aov(total.cte.courses.taken ~ grad.year.10, data = data.10)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/Claude/analysis_non_post_secondary_grads_ct/High school accomplishments/total-cte-courses-taken-10.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)
```
:::

<br>

## Post-secondary

### Summary

Postsecondary attendance shows by far the strongest bivariate association with workforce participation outcomes of any variable examined in this analysis, with a Cramer's V of 0.640 at year 1 — well into strong effect territory and more than double the next strongest variable (ACT participation at V=0.302). The association declines substantially over time (V=0.275 at year 5, V=0.124 at year 10) as the structural constraint driving the year 1 relationship — that postsecondary attendees are by definition in the attending ps category — gradually dissolves. The sample sizes are fixed across all three time points (12,102 never attended; 8,693 attended but did not complete), reflecting that attended.ps is a fixed characteristic rather than a time-varying one.

The year 1 association is largely mechanical in nature. Individuals who attended postsecondary account for the entire attending ps category (52.3% of postsecondary attendees are still attending at year 1), while those who never attended postsecondary have zero representation there by definition. Among those already in the labor market at year 1, postsecondary attendees show notably lower sustained regional employment (15.9% vs. 21.8%), lower sustained statewide employment (5.3% vs. 7.0%), but dramatically lower no MN employment record rates (7.1% vs. 31.6%) and lower non-sustained workforce participation (19.5% vs. 39.6%). This suggests that postsecondary attendees who have exited to the labor market by year 1 are more engaged in formal Minnesota employment than non-attendees, even if not yet at sustained thresholds.

The year 5 table uses a different category structure than years 1 and 10, appearing to reflect an intermediate classification scheme. Reading the available categories, postsecondary attendees show higher meaningful regional employment (20.6% vs. 17.8%) and substantially higher meaningful statewide employment (14.4% vs. 8.8%) compared to non-attendees. Non-attendees show higher rates in the top combined outcome category (31.9% vs. 18.0%) and lower no MN employment record rates (24.8% vs. 19.9%), though the non-standard category labels at year 5 limit direct comparability with years 1 and 10.

At year 10, the association has weakened considerably (V=0.124) but remains meaningful. Postsecondary attendees show higher sustained regional employment (12.0% vs. 9.4%) and notably higher sustained statewide employment (10.1% vs. 5.8%), consistent with the academic pathway pattern where postsecondary exposure — even without completion — is associated with greater out-of-region labor market mobility. Non-attendees show lower no MN employment record rates (15.2% vs. 18.0%), suggesting they are somewhat more likely to remain in Minnesota, though in less stable employment. The largest category at year 10 for both groups is the combined outcome category, with non-attendees showing a substantially higher rate (62.4% vs. 50.8%), reflecting their higher overall Minnesota labor market engagement over the decade.

Overall, postsecondary attendance is the strongest single predictor of workforce participation outcomes at year 1 by a wide margin, though much of that association is structural rather than substantive. Its most durable and interpretable finding — consistent with the academic pathway — is that attending postsecondary without completing is associated with higher sustained statewide employment and lower regional employment relative to never attending, a pattern that persists meaningfully through year 10 and mirrors the trajectory observed for ACT participation, AP exams, and PSEO participation among this non-completer population.

<br>

### `attended.ps`

::: panel-tabset
#### grad.year.1

```{r}
tab_xtab(var.row = data.1$grad.year.1,
         var.col = data.1$attended.ps,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/Claude/analysis_non_post_secondary_grads_ct/Post secondary/attended-ps-1.html")
```

#### grad.year.5

```{r}
tab_xtab(var.row = data.1$grad.year.5,
         var.col = data.1$attended.ps,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/Claude/analysis_non_post_secondary_grads_ct/Post secondary/attended-ps-5.html")
```

#### grad.year.10

```{r}
tab_xtab(var.row = data.1$grad.year.10,
         var.col = data.1$attended.ps,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/Claude/analysis_non_post_secondary_grads_ct/Post secondary/attended-ps-10.html")
```
:::

<br>

# Summary: Bivariate Analysis of Workforce Participation Outcomes Among Non-College Completers

The bivariate analysis examined associations between 21 independent variables and workforce participation outcomes at years 1, 5, and 10 post-graduation among Southwest Minnesota high school graduates who did not complete a postsecondary credential. Variables are ranked separately for categorical predictors (Cramer's V) and continuous predictors (F-statistic from ANOVA) given the absence of a common effect size metric across the two approaches. Rankings reflect general patterns across all three time points, with particular attention to consistency and magnitude of effect.

## **Categorical Predictors Ranked by Effect Size (Cramer's V)**

*1. Postsecondary Attendance* (V=0.640, 0.275, 0.124) is the strongest categorical predictor by a substantial margin, though the year 1 association is largely structural — driven by the mechanical relationship between attending postsecondary and the attending ps outcome category. Its most substantive and durable finding is that postsecondary attendees show persistently higher sustained statewide employment and lower regional employment relative to non-attendees across all three time points, consistent with the academic pathway.

*2. ACT Participation* (V=0.302, 0.155, 0.108) is the strongest high school accomplishment predictor and the strongest variable with a clearly substantive rather than structural association at year 1. Its signature — higher postsecondary attendance, lower regional employment, and substantially higher statewide employment — is the clearest expression of the academic pathway among non-college completers and remains the second strongest categorical predictor at every time point.

*3. AP Exam Participation* (V=0.149, 0.101, 0.065) ranks third, producing a pattern nearly identical to ACT participation in character — high postsecondary attendance early, persistently lower regional and higher statewide employment — but with smaller effect sizes throughout and greater attenuation by year 10.

*4. PSEO Participation* (V=0.186, 0.072, 0.082) ranks fourth overall, with a strong year 1 association driven by an 18-percentage-point postsecondary attendance gap that dissipates rapidly. Its residual finding — a persistent tendency toward statewide rather than regional employment among PSEO participants who did not complete — mirrors the broader academic pathway pattern but at modest effect sizes at years 5 and 10. Note that this variable is restricted to 2011 cohorts and later.

*5. Special Education Status* (V=0.171, 0.107, 0.093) is the strongest demographic predictor, distinguished from other demographic variables by its persistent association with employment instability and reduced statewide labor market mobility across all three time points. Its primary signature is not reduced regional employment but rather elevated non-sustained workforce participation and lower statewide employment that endure through year 10.

*6. English Learner Status* (V=0.140, 0.074, 0.070) ranks sixth, with a strong year 1 association reflecting lower postsecondary attendance and a persistent and growing absence from Minnesota employment records that is its most distinctive feature — differentiating it from other demographic variables and suggesting out-of-state employment, informal work, or geographic departure as plausible explanations.

*7. Economic Status* (V=0.137, 0.083, 0.091) ranks seventh, with a strong year 1 association again driven largely by differential postsecondary attendance. Its most durable finding is modestly higher sustained statewide employment among non-disadvantaged graduates and greater employment instability among disadvantaged graduates, though the association weakens considerably from year 1 to year 5.

*8. CTE Achievement* (V=0.094, 0.106, 0.115) is notable as the only accomplishments variable whose effect size grows at every time point, and the strongest categorical predictor oriented toward regional rather than statewide employment. CTE concentrators and completers show progressively higher regional employment and lower no MN employment record rates relative to the no CTE group, with a 15-percentage-point regional employment gap emerging by year 10.

*9. Non-English Home Language* (V=0.114, 0.068, 0.061) ranks ninth, closely mirroring the English learner pattern in both direction and character but with consistently smaller effect sizes, suggesting that English learner status captures the relevant variation more precisely. Its independent contribution will require evaluation in multivariate models.

*10. EDR* (V=0.093, 0.103, 0.107) is the strongest high school characteristics predictor, with a growing association driven primarily by a widening gap in sustained statewide employment between EDR 6E (Southwest Central) and the other two EDRs — a geographic labor market differentiation that is largely invisible in the regional employment category, which is similar across all three EDRs.

*11. Gender* (V=0.058, 0.087, 0.107) ranks eleventh and is distinctive as the only variable whose effect size increases consistently across all three time points. Its growing association reflects a widening gap in sustained regional employment favoring males and an expanding share of females carrying no MN employment record by year 10, suggesting diverging long-term labor market trajectories by gender.

*12. Race/Ethnicity* (V=0.069, 0.043, 0.054) shows the weakest and least consistent effect sizes of the categorical variables, with American Indian graduates showing a persistently adverse profile and Asian/Pacific Islander graduates displaying a distinctive pattern of high MN record absence alongside relatively high statewide employment among those who remain. Overall effect sizes are small at all three time points.

*13. Community Demographic Description* (V=0.054, 0.078, 0.094) shows statistically significant but practically weak associations, with the most interpretable pattern being a growing statewide employment advantage for urban/town/rural mix graduates and higher MN record absence among entirely rural graduates. Effect sizes remain too small to add meaningful explanatory value beyond other variables.

## **Continuous Predictors Ranked by Effect Size (F-statistic)**

*1. Total CTE Courses Taken* (F=146.8, 101.3, 62.1) produces the highest F-statistics of any continuous variable at all three time points. Its pattern is the mirror image of the academic achievement variables: higher CTE course volume is strongly and consistently associated with sustained regional employment, while the no MN employment record group shows persistently the lowest CTE course-taking at every observation window. The three-tier structure at year 10 — regional employment highest, statewide and non-sustained in the middle, no MN record lowest — is among the clearest patterns in the entire analysis.

*2. MCA Reading Score* (F=144.3, 42.0, 28.0) is the strongest academic achievement measure, consistent with its role as the primary achievement indicator in the full population multivariate models. Its gradient is clear and durable across all three time points: higher scores are associated with statewide employment and — increasingly over time — no MN employment record, while regional employment and non-sustained work form the lowest-scoring tier. F-statistics are the highest of the three MCA measures at years 1 and 5.

*3. ACT Composite Score* (F=114.2, 44.6, 18.3) ranks third among continuous predictors, producing a pattern consistent with MCA Reading: higher scores associated with postsecondary attendance early and statewide employment later, with regional employment consistently at the lowest-scoring tier. The score gap between sustained statewide and sustained regional employment grows from 0.79 points at year 1 to 1.43 points at year 10, indicating increasing score-based stratification across the observation windows. Note this variable is restricted to ACT takers only.

*4. MCA Math Score* (F=89.4, 36.0, 34.6) closely mirrors MCA Reading in pattern but with somewhat lower F-statistics at years 1 and 5. Its year 10 F-statistic (34.6) exceeds MCA Reading (28.0), suggesting math achievement carries slightly stronger differentiation at the later time point. The three-tier consolidation at year 10 — statewide and no MN record as a higher-achieving group, regional and non-sustained as a lower-achieving group — replicates the MCA Reading structure.

*5. MCA Science Score* (F=84.4, 38.1, 25.1) replicates the MCA Reading and Math patterns with high fidelity across all three time points, including the three-tier consolidation at year 10. As in the full population analysis, its voluntary and self-selected nature limits its utility in multivariate models, but its bivariate associations confirm the broader academic achievement gradient and are consistent with retaining MCA Reading as the primary academic achievement indicator.

*6. Average County Unemployment Rate* (F=31.3, 44.5, 54.7) is notable as the only continuous variable whose F-statistic increases substantially over time, reflecting a growing labor market geography effect. Its most consistent finding is that graduates achieving sustained statewide employment disproportionately originate from higher-unemployment counties — consistent with a labor market push dynamic — while the no MN employment record group shows unemployment rate profiles indistinguishable from sustained regional workers, suggesting out-of-state departure rather than intra-state mobility.

*7. Average County Wages as Percent of State Average* (F=35.3, 24.0, 26.1) is statistically significant at all three time points but shows very small absolute differences — all contrasts within one to two percentage points of the state average. Its only durable finding mirrors the unemployment rate variable: graduates in sustained statewide employment consistently originate from lower-wage counties. The negligible absolute magnitude of differences limits its practical significance and supports its exclusion from multivariate models.

## **Cross-Cutting Observations**

Several themes emerge from the full set of bivariate findings. First, the two-pathway structure identified in the full population analysis is fully present among non-college completers: academic indicators (ACT, AP, PSEO, MCA scores, postsecondary attendance) consistently predict statewide labor market mobility, while CTE indicators (CTE achievement, total CTE courses) predict regional attachment — regardless of whether a credential was earned. Second, the no MN employment record category behaves distinctively across the accomplishment variables, increasingly resembling the statewide employment group on academic achievement measures by years 5 and 10, suggesting that a meaningful share of higher-achieving non-completers have left Minnesota rather than failing to engage with its labor market. Third, demographic variables show weaker associations than accomplishment variables across the board, indicating that what students did in high school is more predictive of workforce participation trajectories than who they are. Finally, several variables — community demographic description, county wages, non-English home language — show statistically significant but practically weak associations unlikely to add explanatory value beyond stronger correlated predictors in multivariate models, consistent with decisions made in the full population analysis.

<br>

# What the Data Tell Us: Workforce Participation Among Non-College Completers

For Southwest Minnesota high school graduates who never earned a postsecondary credential, the years following graduation tell two very different stories depending on the path they took through high school.

## **The students who were headed somewhere else**

Some graduates who ultimately did not complete a postsecondary credential were nonetheless oriented toward college from the start. They took the ACT, participated in AP courses and PSEO, earned higher MCA scores, and went on to attempt postsecondary education even if they didn't finish it. What happened to them in the labor market looks a lot like what happened to students who did complete degrees — just a dimmer version of the same pattern. They were more likely to leave Southwest Minnesota for employment elsewhere in the state, and by year 10, a striking share of them had simply disappeared from Minnesota employment records entirely. They weren't failing to find work. The most likely explanation is that they left the state.

This group illustrates something important: academic orientation shapes where people end up even when formal credentials don't follow. The habits of mind, the networks, the postsecondary experience itself — even an incomplete one — appear to pull people toward broader labor markets and away from the region.

## **The students who were built for this place**

The other story belongs to graduates with deep CTE engagement. Higher numbers of CTE courses taken, CTE concentrator or completer status — these are the students who showed up in regional employment at year 1 and stayed there. By year 10, CTE concentrators were nearly twice as likely to be in sustained regional employment as graduates with no CTE engagement at all. This isn't a small finding. It suggests that CTE programs are doing exactly what they are supposed to do for students who engage with them seriously: connecting young people to the regional economy and equipping them to stay.

## **The students who fell through the cracks**

The most troubling pattern in the data belongs to graduates with no CTE engagement and no academic orientation — the students who neither pursued a college track nor developed occupational skills through CTE. By year 10, more than half of graduates with no CTE participation carried no Minnesota employment record at all. This group was already the most likely to have no MN employment record at year 1, and that gap only widened over time. Whether they left the state, work in informal economies, or have simply disengaged from the formal labor market, the data cannot say — but their persistent absence from employment records across a decade is the most concerning finding in the entire analysis.

## **The role of identity and circumstance**

Demographic characteristics — race, gender, economic status, special education status, English learner status — do matter, but they matter less than what students did in high school. Special education students show persistent employment instability. English learners and graduates from non-English speaking homes show elevated absence from Minnesota employment records that grows over time, possibly reflecting employment in sectors that don't generate wage records or communities that maintain strong ties outside Minnesota. Males are more likely to be in sustained regional employment by year 10; females are increasingly likely to carry no MN employment record. These patterns deserve attention, but they operate in the background of the stronger story told by high school experiences.

## **The geography of opportunity**

Where a student went to high school also matters, though modestly. Graduates from EDR 6E — the Southwest Central region anchored by Marshall — show substantially higher rates of employment outside the region compared to graduates from the Upper Minnesota Valley or Southwest EDRs. Graduates from higher- unemployment counties are more likely to leave the region for work elsewhere in the state, consistent with what we would expect when local job markets are tight. But sustained regional employment rates are remarkably similar across the three EDRs, suggesting that the regional economy absorbs similar shares of non-completers regardless of sub-region — it's the path out of the region that differs.

## **The bottom line**

A decade after high school graduation, the non-college completer population has sorted into roughly three groups: those doing steady work in the region, those doing steady work elsewhere, and a large and growing group with no formal Minnesota employment footprint. CTE engagement is the strongest predictor of being in the first group. Academic orientation — even without a credential — predicts the second. And the absence of either, combined with demographic disadvantage, is the clearest signal of the third.

The regional economy depends heavily on workers who choose to stay. This analysis suggests that CTE programs are the most powerful mechanism available for cultivating that attachment among students who will not complete four-year degrees. At the same time, the growing share of non-completers absent from Minnesota employment records by year 10 — many of them higher-achieving graduates who attended postsecondary without finishing — points to an ongoing and unresolved challenge: how to keep talent in a region when the pathways that develop it often point toward somewhere else.
